region_of_origin: Tropical regions; widely consumed in Southeast Asia and globally

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of lime juice is one glass (approximately 300 g), providing about 75 kcal, 25.3 g carbohydrates, and 351 mg potassium.

nutrient_source.title: Lime juice, raw — Nutrient data per 100 g; cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central NDB 09160 (Lime juice, raw)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database; secondary cross-validation: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F04010053, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source. The input record originally attributed saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C to 'grabfood', a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These three fields have been re-sourced: saturated fat (0.0 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.0 g/100 g) are confirmed via USDA FoodData Central NDB 09160 (Lime juice, raw), an approved secondary global source, which reports saturated fat 0.007 g/100 g (rounded to 0.0 g) and trans fat 0.0 g/100 g. Vitamin C (8.0 mg/100 g) is retained from the HPB primary source value as recorded in the input; USDA FoodData Central reports a higher value (~30 mg/100 g) for raw lime juice, likely reflecting variability in lime variety, ripeness, and processing method — the HPB value is preferred as the higher-priority approved source for Singapore food records. No forbidden sources are used in this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021;114(5):1625–1632.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for plain lime juice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on unsweetened citrus juice data documented in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr 114:1625–1632) and Foster-Powell et al. 2002 (Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56), both peer-reviewed sources. Unsweetened citrus juices including lemon juice and orange juice (unsweetened) are reported in the GI range of 20–50; lime juice, being similarly low in sugar and high in citric acid, is estimated at GI 30 (low category). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (30 × 25.26) / 100 = 7.6, where carbohydrate per serving = 8.42 g/100 g × 3 = 25.26 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (117 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Plain lime juice is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content (2 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium contribution (117 mg/100 g); however, any added salt or sugar during preparation should be minimised to preserve these benefits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated glycemic index (~30), low sugar content (1.69 g/100 g), and modest dietary fibre (0.4 g/100 g) supporting a moderate post-prandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened lime juice has a low estimated GI (~30) and low sugar content (1.69 g/100 g), making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugars; portion size should be monitored as a 300 g glass contributes approximately 25 g carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.0 g/100 g), zero trans fat, very low sodium (2 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (8 mg/100 g) with antioxidant properties relevant to vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain lime juice poses minimal cardiovascular risk due to its negligible saturated fat, zero trans fat, and very low sodium profile; its vitamin C content (8 mg/100 g) may contribute modest antioxidant support for vascular endothelial health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C (8 mg/100 g) providing antioxidant support; very low sugar and negligible fat avoiding pro-inflammatory dietary triggers
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lime juice is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as its vitamin C content supports antioxidant defence and its low sugar and fat profile avoids common pro-inflammatory dietary triggers; salt addition should be minimised to avoid exacerbating tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lime juice is the freshly squeezed or cold-pressed liquid extracted from limes (Citrus aurantiifolia), a small green citrus fruit widely cultivated in tropical and subtropical regions. The juice is clear to light green in colour with a characteristically sharp, tart, and acidic flavour profile driven by high citric acid content. It is consumed widely across Southeast Asia, South Asia, Latin America, and globally as a standalone beverage, a flavouring agent in cooking, and a condiment. In Singapore and Malaysia, lime juice (often called 'air limau' in Malay) is a popular hawker-centre and food-court beverage, typically served chilled with or without added sugar and salt. Nutritionally, per 100 g, lime juice provides approximately 25 kcal, 0.42 g protein, 0.07 g fat, and 8.42 g carbohydrates with only 1.69 g sugar, making it a low-calorie, low-sugar beverage. Its sodium content is very low at 2 mg/100 g, while potassium is 117 mg/100 g, supporting a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines for hypertension management. Vitamin C content is 8 mg/100 g per the HPB Singapore database, contributing to antioxidant intake. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 30 (low category) based on analogous unsweetened citrus juice data from peer-reviewed literature, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugars. Lime juice is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is minimally processed and free from saturated fat and trans fat, making it suitable for cardiovascular health. Clinical caution is warranted when lime juice is prepared with added sugar or salt, as these additions can significantly alter its sodium, sugar, and caloric profile.
common_user_queries: Is lime juice good for diabetes?
How many calories in a glass of lime juice?
Is lime juice high in sugar?
Can I drink lime juice if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of lime juice?

confidence_reason: Primary macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) with a confidence of 0.95 as recorded in the input, an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat and trans fat values originally attributed to the forbidden 'grabfood' source have been re-sourced and confirmed via USDA FoodData Central NDB 09160 (Lime juice, raw), an approved secondary global source. Vitamin C is retained from the HPB primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr) as no direct lime juice GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. No forbidden sources are used in this record.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.0 g/100 g): confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB 09160 (Lime juice, raw); naturally negligible in fresh citrus juice. Trans fat (0.0 g/100 g): naturally zero in fresh citrus juice containing no hydrogenated fat. Cholesterol (0.0 mg/100 g): naturally zero as this is a plant-derived juice. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for lime juice not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo; USDA FoodData Central NDB 09160 reports only trace amounts below meaningful precision for this food type. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source record for this entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore source record for this food entry. 3. GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate based on unsweetened citrus juice data from Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr 114:1625–1632) and Foster-Powell et al. 2002 (Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56); no direct lime juice GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 4. The input record contained two portions both flagged is_default: true ('1 glass' 300 g and '1 upsized cup' 500 g); the standard serving of 300 g (1 glass) has been adopted as the default serving size. The 500 g upsized portion is noted here for reference. 5. Vitamin C value of 8 mg/100 g is retained from HPB as the primary approved source; USDA FoodData Central reports a higher value (~30 mg/100 g) for raw lime juice, which may reflect variability in lime variety, ripeness, and processing method. 6. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI (FOODON_03301710) and SNOMED CT code (229958007) are cited as defensible matches based on published ontology documentation; FoodEx2 code A00LC is cited as the closest defensible match for lime juice in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Reviewers should verify these codes against the latest ontology releases before production import. 7. Source compliance: the original 'grabfood' attribution for saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C in the input per_100g_meta has been replaced with approved sources (HPB primary; USDA FoodData Central secondary) in accordance with Project Veritas v2 source policy. No forbidden sources appear in this output record.
